stories.headline: Hezbollah rejects new Israel-Lebanon ceasefire as Israeli strikes continue
stories.body: Hezbollah rejected a US-brokered ceasefire plan agreed by Israel and Lebanon, with leader Naim Qassem calling it humiliating and tantamount to surrender because it requires Hezbollah to halt fire and withdraw operatives from southern Lebanon without Israeli forces first leaving. The agreement, reached in Washington without Hezbollah at the table, would create Lebanese army-controlled “pilot zones” excluding non-state actors and is contingent on a complete cessation of Hezbollah fire, but it includes few details on implementation. Fighting continued despite the announcement, with Israeli strikes reported in southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley and Hezbollah claiming attacks on Israeli soldiers, while Israel said it would continue operations and retain freedom to strike in Lebanon.

stories.headline: Iran studies deal to halt war as US-Iran negotiations remain stalled
stories.body: US-Iran diplomacy remains fragile as Tehran says negotiations have made no progress and continues to link any wider ceasefire to an end to Israeli operations in Lebanon. Trump is weighing a deal to extend the US-Iran ceasefire, reopen discussion of Iran’s nuclear program, and restore the Strait of Hormuz, but Israeli strikes in Lebanon have strained the process and reportedly prompted a tense call between Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu. Trump publicly downplayed the dispute while acknowledging he was “perturbed” by Israel’s continued fighting in Lebanon, underscoring the gap between Washington’s desire to exit the Iran conflict and Israel’s determination to keep targeting Hezbollah.

stories.headline: Ebola outbreak strains central and east Africa as Kenya protests US quarantine plan
stories.body: The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo is worsening amid insecurity, community mistrust, and regional tensions over containment. WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said the Bundibugyo strain may have been spreading since January and that contact tracing remains far below the level needed to control it, while rebel attacks around Beni have killed dozens, displaced residents, and caused confirmed Ebola patients to flee treatment centers. In Kenya, protests erupted over a US plan to quarantine and treat exposed Americans at a military base rather than bring them home, drawing criticism from Kenyan health workers, former US health officials, and experts who say the policy is ethically troubling and could undermine the outbreak response.

stories.headline: Heavy fighting in Mogadishu escalates Somalia election-delay crisis
stories.body: Government forces and opposition-allied fighters exchanged heavy gunfire and mortar fire in Mogadishu after President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud’s term was extended by a year despite opposition claims that the move is unconstitutional. Former leaders Hassan Ali Khaire and Sharif Sheikh Ahmed accused government forces of attacking them as they prepared demonstrations, while police said they were conducting a security operation against armed militias that had launched mortar attacks. Civilians fled several districts as fighting damaged homes and infrastructure, prompting condemnation from the UN, US embassy, EU, and regional bloc Igad, all urging restraint and dialogue over an election roadmap.

stories.headline: Ukraine faces renewed Russian attacks while reporting battlefield gains
stories.body: Russia has renewed heavy bombardment of Kyiv, but Ukrainian and independent battlefield data suggest Moscow is struggling to convert intensified attacks into territorial gains. Analysts cited by La Vanguardia say Ukraine has regained more ground than Russia captured for two consecutive months, aided by expanded drone production that has allowed Kyiv to strike deep into Russian rear areas, logistics nodes, energy infrastructure, and occupied Crimea. Ukrainian leaders argue Russia is showing signs of exhaustion and want greater international pressure for negotiations before winter, while Vladimir Putin continues to insist Russian forces are advancing across the front.

stories.headline: Protests grow in Albania over Jared Kushner-backed luxury resort
stories.body: Thousands of Albanians are protesting a luxury coastal resort project linked to Jared Kushner’s Affinity Partners, arguing it threatens protected wetlands, flamingos, monk seals, sea turtles, and other sensitive habitats around Sazan island and Vjosa-Narta near Zvernec. Environmental groups say work has begun with fencing, private security, bulldozers, and no adequate transparency, public consultation, or permits, while prosecutors have opened an investigation into land-title funds and sales. Prime Minister Edi Rama defends the project as a major investment that will bring jobs and infrastructure, but protesters carrying flamingo symbols and “Albania is not for sale” signs say construction should stop and damaged habitats be restored before any talks.

stories.headline: US House votes to curb Trump’s Iran war powers, drawing Trump’s rebuke
stories.body: The US House voted 215-208 to adopt a war powers resolution seeking to force President Donald Trump to withdraw US forces from hostilities in Iran or obtain congressional authorization, with four Republicans joining Democrats in a rare rebuke. Trump denounced the vote as “unpatriotic” and “meaningless,” while the White House argues Congress is trying to unconstitutionally restrict presidential power. The measure’s legal force is uncertain and enforcement would face Senate, procedural, veto, and court hurdles, but lawmakers and legal experts say the vote sends a significant political signal as the war passes statutory deadlines and negotiations with Iran remain unresolved.

stories.headline: Trump administration separated dozens of migrant children from parents for a second time, AP reports
stories.body: An Associated Press investigation found that dozens of children separated from their parents during Trump’s first administration have been separated again despite a court settlement intended to protect those families through 2031. Some parents have been detained for months or deported after interior immigration arrests, and AP obtained emails indicating that officials sometimes removed people even after discovering they were legally protected from removal. The new separations differ from the 2018 border policy because parents arrested or deported under mass-deportation efforts are often forced to choose whether to leave their children in the United States, prompting advocates to accuse the government of retraumatizing families it had already harmed.

stories.headline: US abortion pill access returns to the courts after ruling blocks mail distribution
stories.body: A federal appeals court ruling that blocked mail distribution of mifepristone, one of the most common abortion methods in the US, has sent abortion access back toward the Supreme Court and revived the issue ahead of the midterms. The 5th Circuit sided with Louisiana in requiring in-person clinic distribution and overriding FDA rules, but mifepristone makers Danco Laboratories and GenBioPro asked the Supreme Court for emergency relief, warning of immediate confusion for time-sensitive medical care. The Supreme Court temporarily restored broad access while it considers the case, leaving providers, patients, and political groups on both sides preparing for a fight over telehealth abortion access and the FDA’s regulatory authority.

stories.headline: Leire Díez affair widens with new audio, UCO searches, and pressure on Guardia Civil leadership
stories.body: The Leire Díez investigation widened as court files, audio recordings, and UCO Guardia Civil reports described alleged efforts to interfere with corruption probes affecting the PSOE, the government, and people close to Pedro Sánchez. Recordings cited by Spanish media show Díez claiming access to Guardia Civil director Mercedes González, while investigators say Díez met with Guardia Civil officers, prosecutors, political figures, businesspeople, and former commissioner José Manuel Villarejo in search of damaging information or procedural leverage. Separately, the UCO searched Tubos Reunidos offices and sought information from the state holding company SEPI in a sealed branch of the case, probing whether a financial operation linked to the Basque company could have diverted money to the alleged network.

stories.headline: David Sánchez denies using family name to influence creation of Badajoz post
stories.body: David Sánchez, brother of Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, denied influencing the creation or award of his job at the Badajoz provincial council during a brief court appearance in a case examining alleged prevarication and influence-peddling. His defense limited questioning and argued that charges tied to his first 2017 contract should be withdrawn because the relevant acts are prescribed, while prosecutors are seeking dismissal and popular accusations led by PP, Vox, and right-wing groups seek prison and disqualification. Former Badajoz council president Miguel Ángel Gallardo also denied tailoring the position for Sánchez, though UCO reports and some testimony have supported the accusation that the role was created specifically for him.

stories.headline: Catalan teachers reject pay agreement and unions call another strike
stories.body: Catalan public school teachers rejected a proposed agreement with the Education Department, with 65.1% voting no in a consultation that drew participation from more than 60,000 educators. The deal included phased salary improvements, recovery of past cuts, additional staff for inclusive education, and new secondary-school chairs, but USTEC, CGT, and Intersindical called a strike for Friday and said the government must reopen negotiations. Education Minister Esther Niubó said the government will still move ahead with the agreement because Professors de Secundària approved it in its own vote, while opposition parties pressed the executive to negotiate further and some unions demanded Niubó’s resignation.

stories.headline: Catalonia budget clears first parliamentary hurdle amid education conflict
stories.body: Catalonia’s 2026 budget passed its first parliamentary hurdle after PSC, ERC, and Comuns voted down total-rejection amendments from Junts, PP, Vox, CUP, and Aliança Catalana. The accounts, worth nearly €50 billion and intended to end years of prorogued budgets, now move to committee amendments before a final vote expected in early July, though the timeline could slip if opponents appeal to the Consell de Garanties Estatutàries. The debate was overshadowed by teachers’ rejection of the education agreement, with ERC and Comuns defending their budget pact as necessary but urging the Illa government to make another effort to resolve the school conflict.

stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV’s Barcelona visit brings transport disruption and dispute over Catalan language use
stories.body: Barcelona is preparing major traffic restrictions and public transport reinforcements for Pope Leo XIV’s June 9-10 visit, with closures around Montjuïc, the cathedral, the Raval, and especially the Sagrada Família, where he will bless the Tower of Jesus. The visit has also sparked controversy over Catalan language use after early liturgical materials suggested the blessing would be in Spanish; Cardinal Joan Josep Omella said the pope will include some Catalan, is already practicing, and has always understood that Catalan is spoken in Barcelona. City and church organizers expect up to 55,000 people across events at the Olympic Stadium, the Sagrada Família, and the cathedral, while officials urge residents to plan travel in advance.
